Craftsmanship 	of
One to Many
Selling
Presentations
How to Assemble 	the Most Effective
Group Selling Presentations for
In-Person Delivery or Media
Module 	4: 	The Close 	and Practical
Details
With 	Dan Kennedy
www.gkic.com
www.getwsodownload.com

by or on behalf of Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle, LLC
and/or 	its 	affiliated 	companies 	(“GKIC”) 	for 	the
purpose of setting out certain confidential information
regarding 	GKIC’s 	business 	activities, 	plans 	and
strategy. 	References to “Presentation” include any
information which has been or may be supplied in
writing or orally by or on behalf of GKIC in connection
with the Presentation or in response to any follow-up
inquiries from the Presentation.
This 	Presentation 	and 	the 	information 	contained
herein are confidential. In addition to the terms of any
confidentiality agreement you may sign with GKIC, by
viewing the Presentation you agree that you and each
of your 	agents, representatives, advisors, directors or
employees (collectively, “Representatives”) will not,
and will not permit any third party to, copy, reproduce
or distribute to others this Presentation, in whole or in
part, at any time without the prior written consent of
GKIC, and that you and all Representatives will keep
confidential 	all 	information 	contained 	herein 	not
already 	in 	the 	public 	domain 	and 	will 	use 	this
Presentation 	for 	the 	sole 	purpose 	of 	familiarizing
yourself with certain limited background information
concerning GKIC and its business strategy, plans and
activities. 	If 	you 	have 	signed 	a 	confidentiality
agreement with GKIC, this Presentation constitutes
Confidential 	Information 	for the 	purposes 	of 	such
agreement. 	If you do not agree to the 	terms of this
Notice, you may 	NOT 	view, copy or distribute any
portion of this Presentation.

believed to be accurate, GKIC has not conducted any

achievement or reasonableness of, and no reliance
should be placed on, any projections, estimates,
forecasts, analyses or forward-looking statements
contained 	in this Presentation which involve by their
nature 	a 	number 	of 	risks, 	uncertainties 	or
assumptions 	that 	could 	cause 	actual 	results 	or
events to differ materially from those expressed or
implied in this Presentation. 	Except to the extent
otherwise 	indicated, 	this 	Presentation 	presents
information as of the date hereof. 	The delivery of
this Presentation shall not, under any circumstances,
create any implication that there will be no change
in the affairs of GKIC after the date hereof. 	In

and undertakes no obligation to update any of the

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	4
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
One to Many Selling Presentations: 	The Close
So here we are finally at closing and 	there 	are 	different kinds of 	Closes.
Different Kinds of Closes
There’s obviously different things we’re going to close on with a presentation,
one is all we’re going for is an appointment 	– 	a 	next step commitment. So we’ll
talk about that first.
For Lead 	/ 	Permission Response
For 	lead generation responses, 	in a room or on a teleseminar or on a webinar,
often it pretty much only requires a 	very clear instruction.
Specific to the financial advisors, 	for example, 	a lot of times the reason they
wind 	up with 	as 	few appointments is because they weren’t 	very clear:
“Now we’re going to get 	your 	appointments booked. 	Here’s the forms. 	Susan is
back there, take your form to Susan and Susan is
going to set your appointment sometime in the
next four days and 	she’ll 	give you the shortlist of
things it would be ever so nice if you brought it
with you to your appointment.”
Often they’re not that direct and explicit.
One 	of the things to keep in mind 	in 	all of this, is one of the things people are
pretty good at 	generally speaking is following direct instructions.
They’re very good at standing in line, they’re great at that. 	I’m not but most
people are really good at 	it.
I’m amazed when I go places and 	I see 	they’re going to stand… 	this 	Monkees
concert 	I was at, 	I mean there’s a million people in line to get in, and they’re
being 	bossed around and they’re in lines.
People 	are 	generally
pretty good at
following direct
instructions.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	5
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
You would think people would mutiny at this. I just went and threw some
money in 	a 	slot machine and waited until it was one minute before time to start
and 	there was no line; then you went in. 	But 	they’re in line for 30 minutes. 	Why?
They’ve 	got 	a 	reserved 	seat. 	Where 	is it going to go? I don’t 	get it.
Most people are pretty good at following explicit instructions.
Often the failure is just we didn’t 	give them explicit instructions.
Second, 	alternate 	choice 	is usually better than single choice.
So 	it’s 	the 	appointment 	with 	choice 	of 	date 	and 	time; 	it 	might 	be 	the
appointment or 	“If you’re not yet ready to schedule an appointment, we 	want to
get you 	booked for a diagnostic call on the phone with one of our coaches. So
you want to get under the blue flag with your form.”
You have to decide 	how much
information 	you’re 	going 	to
request in this environment.
Obviously the more information
you 	request, 	the 	bigger 	the
impediment 	to 	scheduling 	a
meeting. 	The 	less information,
the less the impediment.
So 	that’s a facet you have to decide.
If 	your goal in life is the most number of appointments out of the room 	– 	or 	off
the webcast to diagnostic calls 	– 	as 	humanly possible, 	and 	you’ve 	got a bunch of
sales people who need to be fed and they could get blood out of a rock, 	that’s
one thing.
If you’ve 	only got like one good sales person or you’re doing it 	and you only
want to be on the phone or in front of a super-qualified 	prospect, 	you’ve 	got to
For Lead/Permission Response
 	Simplest 	– 	merely requires CLEAR
instructions
 	Alternate choices
 	How much info to request?
 	Reassurances v. reasons not to
 	Urgency v. procrastination
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	6
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
be okay with driving 80% of them 	away and not having them register in order to
get the ones who do register to fill out 	a 	two-page form.
Something interesting… at 	the current version of the Phill 	Grove “Get Rich 	in
Real Estate” 	weekend seminar, there’s three or 	four different things sold but
then the last thing sold is the $100,000 two-year big dog thing.
They actually go in and meet one-to-one and somebody closes them. Early
results, 	only three cities, 	but we’ve nearly doubled the closing percentage by
forcing them to fill out a four-page questionnaire to go in the room and sit down
with the closer.
You’ve 	got to decide on 	“reassurances” 	versus 	“reasons not to”.
The 	“no salesman will call” 	copy has been around since there was a phone.
You’ve 	got 	to decide… 	again you don’t want to make promises 	you won’t keep…
but 	“If you come in to meet with us, 	we will never pursue 	you, 	we will never chase
you, 	we won’t follow up with you. 	Our discussion either makes sense to you and
you ask how to go forward…”
So 	you’ve 	got to decide 	whether 	you want to 	be
that reassuring.
Ultimately 	you’ve 	got 	to 	deal 	with
procrastination versus urgency. 	You’ve 	got to
think about how 	we 	can get them to do this right
now, and there’s either got to be scarcity, 	there’s
got to be exclusivity, 	there’s got to be danger of
loss from waiting, there’s got to be a bribe that
they want.
The 	one thing I will tell you 	is that, 	even when all you are doing in a physical
room is booking an appointment, it is better to have some 	“thing” 	that they walk
out of there with than just an appointment slip.
We’ve nearly
doubled the closing
percentage by
forcing them to fill
out a four-page
questionnaire.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	7
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
Just as you will sell more product if you have the product piled up, you will book
more appointments if you have something piled up that they get to take home
with them.
When you are B2B 	and you are serious, I would make it a relevant useful gift
rather 	than I would 	a toy 	gift 	– 	a 	tool of reference but not something they have
to get through before the appointment.
In some other environments, 	it’s the 	“nice gift” 	– 	“We’ve got a limited number of
these 	‘I’m a patriot’ 	Ronald Regan coffee mugs. 	So the 	first 10 old people who come
back 	and get their appointment taken care of with a 	financial advisor, you get this
cup and you get a bib with a flag on it…”
For An Appointment
The 	other thing you need to know about driving them to appointments, one they
need to know 	what to expect 	at the appointment.
So 	again to use 	the 	example 	of 	the 	financial advisor, one of the reasons 	they
muck it 	up and they don’t get as many appointments as they think they should,
is because they sort of leave it vague what’s going to happen when you come to
the appointment.
Nobody likes 	uncertainty and they will generally imagine
something more onerous and worse and difficult than it is.
They need to know exactly what’s going to 	happen to 	them and what won’t
happen to them when they come to the appointment or the diagnostic 	call 	–
which is usually the alternative if we’re selling this at a distance that you want
them to do.
Secondly, 	they 	should be aware
of some 	benefits 	that occur by
the call or the appointment, 	that
For An Appointment
 	What 	to 	expect
 	Benefits 	of
 	Alternate choices
 	How much info to request?
 	Reassurances v. reasons not to
 	Urgency v. procrastination
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	8
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
occur whether or not they ever go any further than that or not.
They’re giving up time so it would 	be cool if, 	even if I don’t like you and I don’t
want to do business with you after we meet or after I talk to you on the phone, I
know 	something 	I 	didn’t 	know 	before 	that 	I 	can 	do 	something 	with
independently.
For A 	Purchase
For purchase, it’s not that different.
Clearly Understood Offer
A 	lot of group presentations 	fall apart because people
are just so confused about the offer itself.
They don’t know is it a seminar, 	is it 	a 	home study course, 	is it a 	pill, 	is it
surgery… what 	the hell is it?
We want a good 	clearly understood offer and, 	any environment in which you
can do it, 	have a good order form. 	Halbert’s rule for print 	was 	you ought to
spend as much 	time 	on the order form as 	you 	spend on 	the 	sales letter.
If you can control it, 	at the back of the room, 	not only do you want a really good
order form but you want a really good crew.
The biggest, 	worst 	thing about
the Peter Lowe 	events 	was 	we
had 	bad crews.
So 	you’re 	driving 	them 	back
there 	and 	you’ve 	got 	terrible
people 	– 	well 	anything 	from
mediocre to terrible, it varied
but you didn’t 	have good.
For Purchase
 	Clearly understood offer
 	“Irresistible” 	offer(s)
 	Risk reversal
 	Ease of Buying
 	Urgency
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	9
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
Tommy 	Hopkins flew five in; 	he put five of his people there on top of everybody
else’s people just to shove them out of the way and do it.
So a 	good order form that restates the offer, 	the components, 	the value build 	– 	if
you relied 	heavily on value build.
Irresistible Offer
The 	better the offer, 	once you finally get to it, 	the better the results you’re going
to get.
Irresistible offers are all about the structure of price 	to value.
Are 	we going to show price? 	Are 	we going to show individual price items that
add up to some malarkey number that is then deeply discounted with 	some
reason why?
J Douglas Edwards called it the reduction to the ridiculous.
You 	can sometimes do it by individual pricing; 	sometimes 	you can do it 	by
apples 	to 	oranges comparisons 	– 	“Here’s a box with 10 seminars in it and if you
came to all 10 seminars, 	you would have spent $36,412 plus air fares 	and hotel
rooms 	and the whole thing is $499.” We 	didn’t compare apples to apples we
compared apples 	to oranges.
Price versus desire 	– 	what’s the value of the desire? We 	can really talk about
what will it be worth 	to you? 	MasterCard has this 	“priceless” 	campaign they run;
it’s 	the 	most 	successful 	ad
campaign they’ve ever run. The
value of this is priceless.
Gift with 	purchase 	and 	toy in
box. 	Gift with 	purchase 	almost
always helps an offer because
people 	like 	getting 	something
for nothing.
“Irresistible” Offers
 	Price v. 	Value
 	Price v. Desire
 	GWP
 	THE toy in the cereal box
 	Speed of 	gratification
 	“Tail” (reassurance)
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	10
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
They intellectually know 	it’s not 	something 	for nothing 	but it 	doesn’t make any
difference; 	everybody is stimulated by the idea of getting something 	for nothing,
so 	gift with 	purchase almost always should be in an offer.
The toy in a box 	– 	if 	you’re my age, 	you grew up not only with the toy in 	the
Cracker 	Jack box, 	you grew up with toys in the 	cereal box.
Most 	cereals 	completely made 	out 	of sugar that children might eat had toys in
them, sometimes they weren’t 	very 	good toys but they were toys and the cereal
company put them at the bottom of the box. You either ate it or you dumped it
out into 	eight bowls… I only did that once… 	and then put them back in the
cupboard 	in order to get to 	the toy.
The toy in the cereal box is the thing they want
more than anything else.
When you create the bonus, 	the gift with the purchase that is the one thing that
they really want. If 	I’m selling the entire 	Magnetic 	Marketing 	system to dentists
or chiropractors 	– 	just to pick a group 	– 	I 	am now
these days in an uphill battle.
You 	can actually feel the air deflate in the room, I
had them 	right until I got to 	“Tools you have to
use and things you have to learn.” 	Then you
could just feel the palpable disappointment in
the room.
There’s ways to fix it that I’m unwilling to do but at least I can do the 	“… and I
have this cool 	‘appointment 	no 	sale’ 	letter sequence that 	a 	chiropractor used 	and
they 	get 18% of the patients they 	don’t 	get to accept 	case 	treatment to 	come back.
I’m going 	to give you these three letters and you can use them exactly as you
want.”
Everybody is
stimulated by the
idea of getting
something for
nothing.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	11
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
Well that’s what they really want in the first place. 	They 	just want something
they don’t have to learn, 	do anything with, 	write their names on, hand out
preferably 	– 	not 	even send out 	– 	if 	we can just hand it out in the office that
would be better.
Way 	back 	with Joe Polish, in 	his 	kit sale, we determined that what they really
wanted to do was see Joe do a carpet audit and a pitch. 	So 	we took that out of
the product and we 	made it the gift with purchase for the top level, 	the 	B 	offer
package.
In 	Magnetic Marketing 	at the Lowe 	events, 	I think it was the package 	of 	special
reports which were all very specific 	– 	one 	of which was how to get good mailing
lists, 	which I had set up 	a 	big desire for 	lists 	and
it’s 	something nobody knows how to do.
They wanted that secret and it was the critique
coupons because what they really wanted was 	to
send 	their 	shit 	to me, 	have me fix it 	and 	send it
back to them 	– 	that’s what they really want.
So you’ve got gift with 	purchase and then you’ve 	got toy in the box; 	the toy in
the box is the one thing they really want that will make them grudgingly buy
and eat everything else in order to get it.
You want to figure out what that is.
Speed 	of gratification. 	When we 	sell, 	say to a financial advisor, 	the big 	whizz-
bang 	documentary film thing done on you and exclusive in your area, the big
problem with that sale is you can’t get a documentary film tomorrow.
It’s probably going to be a few months by the time you actually have a 	thing 	you
can use.
There’s no speed in this thing, 	there’s no immediate gratification.
They just want
something they don’t
have to learn 	or 	do
anything with.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	12
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
But 	there’s three or 	four things 	we 	do, 	much less elaborate that actually can get
done pretty quick, like in 	a matter of days, 	and 	then 	another one in a couple of
weeks.
We can take two or three of those things and space them out, one 	that’s now,
one that’s in a few days and one that’s in a few weeks 	– 	to 	give people some
gratification while they wait for the thing that they actually bought.
It’s the same reason it sells 	more if you’ve 	got
the stuff there… 	if you are selling through a
webcast 	or 	a 	webinar 	and 	you’re 	going 	to
deliver 	hard 	product 	– 	which 	I 	highly
recommend 	– 	you may also want something that
is immediately available digitally or that is the
impending event, 	that tomorrow night 	is 	the fast
start class and everybody is coming to fast start
tomorrow.
You 	want something that is fast.
You will always 	help an offer 	– 	it 	comes with pain 	but 	you will always help an
offer if you attach a 	tail 	to it. It’s very reassuring.
Even 	though 	the 	American 	Writers 	and 	Artists’ 	“Certified 	Copywriter 	for
Information Marketers” 	course is as complete as it could possibly 	be 	– 	it’s all
there if you want, 	as a 	freelance writer, 	to target info-marketers 	and 	get them as
clients 	– 	the 	sale is infinitely helped if there’s going to be two live Q&A calls with
Dan.
Giving you the calls 	on CD that happened before 	is 	not as 	reassuring.
It’s very reassuring for the person to think, “I’m going
to be able to get my questions answered.”
If 	you’re going to
deliver hard product,
you may also want
something that is
immediately
available digitally.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	13
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
Now, 	as a practical matter by the way, 	two-thirds of the people don’t show up
for the calls and a third of the people that do, 	most of them are 	too shy to ask a
question. You 	really wind up answering questions for three people.
But you still have to do the damn calls; 	so the tail is now recreating time and
manual labor to something that does not require it in order for it to work, 	but
it’s very, 	very useful in 	the sale.
When I 	did them 	for Magnetic 	Marketing and another product 	– 	which I do not
do now 	– 	the 	critique 	coupon drove a lot of sales. 	I know because 	anecdotally
people would say it to you, this is the thing they waited to ask about.
“Now 	let me get this straight, 	I get this coupon and I can send you stuff and you
will personally get it.” “Yes, that’s exactly how it works.”
That tail drove a lot of the purchase activity and fortunately 	there was 	60 	– 	70%
breakage, 	no redemption. The 	30% redemption that did occur is painful, I mean
painful and not much backside benefit. 	Every 	once in a while I’d spot a 	“whale” 	–
somebody 	who 	I could 	pick off as a client 	so that made me feel a little better
about it. But 	there were some months I’m doing 300 critiques.
You 	can standardize some of it but basically if Susan has sent in a nine-page
sales letter about selling persuasive voice training to plumbers 	and she 	has
really butchered it 	– 	I 	mean it really needs help 	– 	no check marks on 	a 	form are
going to do.
If you really want to keep that promise, 	I’ve 	got to write a page or two to Susan.
It 	took time but it drives a lot of sales.
Risk Reversal
Great offers have great 	risk 	reversal. 	The 	more of the sense of risk you take
away, 	the more you can drive the sale.
My 	overarching advice about that is don’t make guarantees
that you’re going to be grumpy about honoring.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 13 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	14
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
Be willing to honor them even when the person is wrong but then 	make the
boldest best guarantee that you possibly can.
Understand guarantees, risk reversal is all math. 	None 	of it is emotion, thus you
cannot be emotional about it. 	It’s just math, 	that’s all it is.
Does it 	lift sales enough to more than justify its expense? 	If it does, 	end of story.
Now 	how wrong Susan 	is when she wants a refund and how late she is and how
she only sent 	half the stuff back and we can find her at eBay having made 56
copies… none of that matters. 	It’s math, 	that’s all it is.
If, in 	your category you think there’s
no sense of risk, 	you’re wrong.
Whatever it is you do, 	people are frightened of you and they’re frightened of
themselves.
They 	either sense risk in doing business with you
or they sense risk in their ability to then do 	/ 	use
/ 	benefit from the thing that you are selling them.
There’s 	a store in Amish country about 	an 	hour drive 	from here; it’s 	worth
visiting, it’s this huge campus called Lehman, 	it’s incredible.
They 	sell 	to 	the Amish but they sell to everybody, so 	they have some stuff in
there you would never want but it’s kind of cool to see it 	– 	like 	they’re selling a
coal-powered refrigerator.
There’s everything in this 	store and you can spend two 	or 	three hours. There’s a
restaurant 	where 	you can have lunch; 	I mean it’s like a day trip. They 	have a
catalog 	and they have a website.
We’re down there 	and one of the things that they had for sale 	is 	they got this
thing, I have no idea if it works. 	They’ve 	got this thing that fakes out wasps
Make 	the boldest
best 	guarantee that
you possibly can.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 14 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	15
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
somehow. 	You 	hang it on your deck and it looks like a wasp nest; 	you just pop it
and it hangs down and you hang it in the pitch.
The box says, “Wasps think this is a wasps’ 	nest and they don’t want to get in 	a
confrontation with other wasps, because they’re territorial and respect each
other’s territory. 	They see this thing and they leave you alone.”
So 	I buy two of them and right behind me there’s two people looking at it, and
the wife says to the husband, 	and I’m sure she’s right, “Oh you’ll never hang
those up, 	leave them alone” 	and they walk away.
Now 	mine, 	I’ve had them for 	two 	years and I haven’t hung 	them up.
See, 	I think I’m going to hang them up but a lot of people say to themselves, “I’m
never going to use that,” or “I’m never going to do that,” or “I wouldn’t know
how to hang it up.”
If you think that’s not going on with what you sell, if it is going on with
something as simple as a fake wasp nest that you take out of the box and go
whoop, it’s going on with everything.
So 	don’t think you shouldn’t have risk reversal.
Ease of Buying
When you do presentation by however you do a
presentation, you want to be really careful about
where 	now 	their 	attempt 	to 	buy 	is 	getting
mucked 	up.
A 	lot of people don’t check. The 	entire time I was
on 	the 	road, 	I called to make sure the phone was working 	a 	lot. I didn’t really
want to talk to anybody, 	I just wanted to make sure it was working,
I’d 	get to the hotel 	and, 	when I was checking in, 	I’d send a fax just to 	make sure it
went through because I want to know my fax machine is working.
You 	want to be
really careful about
where their attempt
to buy is getting
mucked up.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 15 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	16
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
Because I don’t trust anybody but me to make sure this thing is working, I’m still
paranoid about these 	sorts of things.
The 	Peter Lowe sales 	thing at the 	back was horrible; 	if you drive them to phones
and you aren’t controlling the phones…
The ease of buying starts with who they have to deal with
besides you 	– 	what they have to do to get an order placed.
I was the only one 	at the 	Success events, 	the entire 	nine years I was there, 	who
used my own order form.
Gradually Peter switched everybody to one big order form with everybody’s
stuff on it in two point invisible, and six NCR copies and they filled it out and
they swiped 	the cards… not me.
I’ve 	got an order form and you just go back and you hand the order form in 	and
you get your bag. We’re not 	swiping your card, 	we’re not doing any of that crap.
“Quick, 	simple, here!”
I always said, 	as long as you get me a phone number, 	you can screw up
everything else 	– 	your 	address can be wrong, 	you can be dyslexic, 	you can spell
the name wrong. 	I don’t give a shit we can fix it.
Just get me a phone number, 	get their bag, 	get them out of the way so the next
person can buy 	– 	because there’s 300 people standing in line and they wanted 	to
go 	home 	an hour and half ago.
The ones at the back of the line are walking away,
I did a seminar many years ago 	– 	a 	church marketing seminar for pastors. I 	was
a featured speaker at a two-day event on church marketing 	and 	I’m 	having lunch
with a guy by the name of Jack Boland, who has 	passed away since.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 16 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	17
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
There’s 	six or seven of us at the table and Jack says, 	“Here’s a marketing problem
for you. We 	have somebody come in and speak and 	we’ve 	got 3,000 people there
and we’ve 	got their stuff in the bookstore.
We 	create a stampede but the bottleneck is the bookstore and people won’t wait
because they want to go have fried chicken and they want to go do whatever they
want to do after church.
They all run there but they see the 	bottleneck and they say, ‘Well I’ll get it later,’
and off they go. So 	what do we do about that?”
I’m being 	a wise ass, 	so I’m going to entertain myself. With a completely straight
face, 	I say 	“Well you know what they just started to do at the rent-a-car 	places?
Now you don’t have to go in to check out; 	some person comes out on roller-
skates with a little 	doohickey 	and skates 	up to
you and swipes your card and sends you on your
way.”
I’m being 	an ass and I think no more about it
until, 	a month or so later, 	I get pictures in the
mail; he’s got 	people on roller-skates with 	little doohickeys 	skating up and down
the line running your card and 	fetching your stuff.
This 	is hilarious. 	I give him all the credit in the world.
I was the only one at the Peter Lowe 	events 	– 	I 	had to bribe extra money 	– 	who
got 	people out from behind the tables to get 	them 	out into the lines to 	go to 	the
back of the lines and start getting their order forms and running up and getting
their stuff 	– 	so 	we don’t lose the back part of the line.
The first thing about this is let’s beware of the sales prevention department.
We 	create this rush of activity and then whoever is next in the way is figuring
out how to stop people from buying.
Beware of the sales
prevention
department.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 17 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	18
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
The first 	time I heard it, 	I was speaking in an event and a person comes to the
table, I didn’t think this up myself 	– 	you 	don’t see it on our order forms now, 	you
did see it on mine during the 	recession years.
I’ll 	do it now with 	a 	big ticket item, 	you’ll still see it occasionally, but I learned it
from a customer. It didn’t occur to me 	but 	it should have because I watched my
mother do this for years.
So a 	customer comes to the table 	and I happened to overhear it, it wasn’t a 	Peter
Lowe 	event, 	it was years before that, “Can I 	split this purchase on three credit
cards?”
Of 	course, 	the sales 	prevention department says, “No.”
I 	go, “Yes, 	come with me. Now what exactly…?
“Well 	I know exactly how much I’ve 	got on this one and I know how much I’ve
got available on this one and I don’t have enough on any one of them 	but if I 	use
all three of them…”
“Great, 	I got it. Here. We’ll 	figure this out.”
Then 	I started putting it on order forms 	– 	here, 	you can tell us, 	credit card 	–
charge this much to it, 	credit 	card 	– 	charge this much to it, 	credit card 	– 	charge
this much to it.
I did it all through the 	Success 	Track years. You’d 	be amazed how many 	dentists
split a $399 purchase into, “Put this much on this credit card, 	this much on this
credit card…”
It should 	have occurred to me. I watched 	my mother do it for years, little notes
on the back of all 	the 	credit cards keeping track of how much was left and
available credit 	– 	like 	25 credit cards and there was $18 on this one and there’s
$42 on this one and she wasn’t alone.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 18 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	19
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
The corporate 	answer to that is 56 reasons why we can’t do that, “We 	can only
have one credit card for one purchase and we have to swipe it.”
So 	beware of the sales prevention activity that gets in your way.
When you’re selling to people over and over again, 	if you can just say, “We have your
credit card on file, 	just say 	‘yes,’” why not do that?
Terms
There’s a whole issue about whether you want to do installment payments,
whether you want to do two-pay, whether you want to do three-pay.
In 	some cases, 	you can hide it so you can get 	as many sales as you can in one-pay
and 	then 	you can go back to everybody who didn’t buy who were at the
presentation or who watched the webcast or who were on the teleseminar. 	You
can then give them the two-pay, 	the three-pay, the four-pay, the 52fifty-two-pay
option.
In other cases, 	where you can’t, 	people fear the trade down. 	They 	fear if I say to
you, 	“You can do it in three-pay,” 	now everybody will do it 	in 	three-pay.
This is a false fear 	– 	now 	it will vary by the audience, 	I will grant you that; 	but
my experience is, if I do three-pay and I don’t 	have 	any incentive even to 	pre-
pay, a 	huge number of people 	still do it as one-pay. They 	don’t want to screw
around with installment payments.
The money 	item 	is not that big to them 	and 	the kind of person
they are is that they don’t buy on installments.
Most of the time the trade 	and 	increased 	revenue is worth far more than the
hassle 	and even 	what 	little trade down occurs. If you think your number is a
significant number to the people you’re selling to 	– 	not a prohibitive 	barrier
number, 	just a significant number…
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 19 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	20
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
There are different significant numbers for you, 	for me, 	for 	Trump. 	There’s some
number which, 	for something, 	we 	hesitate. If 	it’s that number, 	you may want to
do two-pay or three-pay.
You also want to think about price structure if
you 	are 	doing 	A/B offers where you do the
gambit 	where the amount 	is the same just the
number of payments 	– 	so the 	basic offer is two
payments of 	$200, 	the deluxe offer 	is four payments of 	$200.
The 	person is still just making a $200 choice, all you’ve done is extend the
number of payments.
Overall, 	the important thing is 	to 	make buying as easy as possible when you are
group 	selling.
Whether online or in a room, 	make it as easy as you possibly can for them to get
the transaction done and, 	if it’s big numbers, 	as fast they possibly can.
If 	they even just wander off to the restroom,
they may not come back.
If they don’t get taken 	care of pretty quick, 	now somebody is trying to talk them
out of it. It’s 	why, 	in most sized 	groups, 	if I’m selling from the stage, 	I want order
forms passed out.
I don’t want them going to the back to 	get an order form because everything
moves faster if they have an opportunity to fill it out at the table while I’m
finishing the pitch and well they’re moving.
Urgency
People will wait. There is “believed scarcity” and the 	keyword here is believed.
You 	have to find ways to make it believable.
If 	you sell to people repetitively via group presentations 	– 	or 	really by any other
media 	– 	you 	have to act in a way that does not have them stop believing you.
The important thing
is make buying as
easy as possible.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 20 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	21
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
So believed 	scarcity, 	believed 	exclusivity, 	believed 	price change if they wait,
believed 	deadlines; 	a reason why for the above.
You can’t 	say, “You can only have it if you do it right now and you can never
have it if you don’t do it right now” if 	you don’t have a reason for that. 	Otherwise
you won’t be believed or you’ll just be thought of as a mean, 	nasty, 	cruel SOB.
The decisive action argument or other status argument 	– 	if 	you can make acting
now a 	self-esteem reinforcing thing and you can make not acting now the
equivalent of going and standing under the white flag with 	a “Big 	Loser” 	on your
forehead, you will get more people to act now.
So 	you 	can do a 	wrap about the power of decisive action. Foster, 	because he
talked about 	Napoleon Hill a lot in the Success 	Track seminars, 	he used the 	story
that unbeknownst to 	Napoleon Hill, 	Andrew Carnegie had a stopwatch under
the desk and 	he 	gave Napoleon Hill only 60 seconds to say 	‘yes’ 	or 	‘no’ 	to his
offer.
If Napoleon Hill had not been a decisive individual, 	history itself would be
changed and you would not have 	read a copy of “Think and Grow 	Rich” and 	we
would never 	have 	known 	the 17 laws of success.
He 	would do this whole wrap
about Andrew Carnegie and the
stopwatch. 	I have no idea if it’s
real or not; 	I don’t know, 	I don’t
care, 	it 	doesn’t 	make 	any
difference to me.
You can build a wrap that makes
being 	decisive 	a 	self-esteem
affirming act, so you want to put
that in there.
Urgency
 	Believed scarcity
 	Believed exclusivity
 	Believed price
 	Believed deadlines
 	“Reason Why” for above
 	The Decisive Action Argument OR
Other Status Argument
 	Impending Event(s)
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 21 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	22
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
Of 	course, you 	can use impending events, 	“By buying this, 	you’re 	coming to this,
you’re benefiting from this and it’s happening tomorrow 	/ 	next week 	/ 	today.”
When 	we group sell area-exclusive programs, 	at the end of the 	Discovery 	Day,
Fast Start day is tomorrow. You buy now, 	you’re going to the 	Victory Dinner 	and
then you’re doing 	Fast Start 	tomorrow.
If you 	don’t buy now, 	you’re going home and you’re not going to dinner. Out!
That’s loser 	/ 	winner and 	it’s impending event.
In a 	Physical Environment
In a 	physical environment, 	you pretty much want to block easy exit with
product tables.
Ideally you want them in the room not outside
the room. 	If they’re outside the room, 	they’re set
up 	in a way that doesn’t make it easy to get to the
john or get out of the door of the hotel.
You don’t want to let them stick it all the way
down in their bookstore on the second floor, 500 miles away from the room that
you’re in. 	It 	makes 	a 	difference.
That’s the reason why you can’t 	get 	off the 	Disney attraction without walking
through 	the gift shop. 	Casinos are 	laid out 	so you’ve 	got to snake 	to get to a
restroom past slot machines and gaming and bars. You can’t just walk right to
the restroom from wherever you are. 	There is never a direct path to a restroom
in a casino from any place, you can’t get there.
They 	didn’t do that because they 	were 	drunk when they designed the place.
They did it because, 	going or coming, 	they’ve 	got a chance of you stopping and
dumping some money in a machine.
So 	where you put the sales activity matters. If you are in a big event, 	like the
Lowe 	events where I was, 	there are 	great virtues 	of being 	last; 	we would have
If there are multiple
locations, use you to
draw everybody 	to
one place.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 22 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	23
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
eight product sales areas 	– 	up 	on the second floor of the arena, 	on the ground of
the arena 	– 	it was 	a mess.
Most of the time I could get them to shut them all down and come to one on the
floor during 	the time I was on stage because I was last. 	So I could 	“pied 	piper”
everybody to 	one place.
If 	there are multiple locations, 	use yours 	to draw everybody as much as possible
to one place. 	“You saw them all day long folks, 	they’re still there; 	there are six
places you can go get this stuff. 	I’ll be at 	that 	one; 	come see me and we’ll talk.”
Strong expectations 	conveyed, 	again tell people what the hell they’re supposed
to do. Don’t be shy when you get to the close, “Here’s what’s going to happen
now…”
I will almost always, 	if I’m going to hang around somewhere in a physical
environment, say, 	“I’m happy to stay here and answer questions with you, 	talk to
you as long as you would like. 	But go take care of business first. 	Get 	your stuff, 	then
come see me.”
Mostly 	they’ll go do that. 	The 	ones who don’t, 	they won’t buy period so be rude
to them and get them out of the
way 	before 	the 	people 	that
bought get there.
You 	want 	a 	stampede. 	A
physical 	stampede 	causes
people to join the stampede.
You’ve 	got to get them moving.
You 	just have to. 	If 	they’re not moving, 	it’s difficult to close the 	sale.
I saw Foster do it once; 	it’s the most amazing thing I’ve ever seen. 	I would never
have had the balls, today 	I wouldn’t 	have 	the balls to do it.
In a Physical Environment
 	Product Table blocking easy exit
 	ONE Product Area
 	Strong expectation, conveyed verbally
and visually
 	Stampede
 	2nd bite at the apple
 	Assumptive language 	– 	during pitch
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 23 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	24
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
It was 	about 25 docs in 	a 	room; 	he turns them lose, 	they’re supposed to come
back and get their stuff, they’ve 	got forms. 	He’s done 	the “I’m filling the form out
now 	with my pen and my American Express card 	and 	you take out 	yours.”
These 	people have done nothing. 	They don’t get up at the break; 	they sit there.
I’m in the back; 	I’ve never seen it before, 	haven’t seen 	it since. Nobody moves.
He 	marches slowly, 	graciously, 	genteelly to the back, 	gets a bundle of stuff,
marches all the way back 	up 	to the front to the furthest guy at that table and
puts it in front of him. Walks all the way back… I’m serious 30 times… 	puts it
down, 	goes 	back, 	gets another one, 	puts it down…
Everybody is watching him, 	nobody is moving. 	I’m standing in the back thinking,
“Damndest thing I ever saw.” 	I don’t know where
it came from; 	I have no idea. 	“It will be really
interesting to see what happens. It’s probably
not going to be good but it will be interesting.”
So 	he does this 30 times; 	it takes forever and
finally 	he 	walks back to the back 	and stands
there.
100%. One after another.
Filled 	the form 	out, 	came back, 	handed in the form 	and 	nobody said a damn
word. They 	went to 	the 	john, 	they came back, 	they 	sat down 	and 	they 	all had
their stuff. I 	never saw anything like 	it.
But usually, 	stampede; 	they join the stampede.
You’ve 	got to think through, “How 	am 	I going to cause a stampede?”
Scarcity, 	with 	the 	gift 	with 	purchase; 	we only have this number of this; only the
first 35 people are going to get the tail calls. 	You need the stampede.
You will always sell
more because people
will join the
stampede.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 24 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	25
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
You 	will always sell more because people will join the stampede; 	they don’t like
to be the dummy sitting there while everybody else is running.
Second bite at the apple, 	structure your sales
opportunity, 	your speaking opportunity, 	if 	you
can, 	so 	that you come back after the selling
break.
I never had the opportunity to do that at 	the
Lowe 	events but in most other environments 	– 	and 	the ones I build 	– 	you 	could
do that. 	So the 	main sales break, 	when people go back and get their stuff, 	you
want them coming back to the seats with the stuff.
Now you’re here and you’ve 	got stuff and you’re looking at it 	and you’re taking it
out of the bag, 	and he’s got stuff and the little annoying guy hasn’t got any stuff.
He’s trying to look at their stuff and he kind 	of gets the message.
Then, 	in your next segment, 	you can just be dumb. You 	can speak and act as if
everybody has got the stuff. 	“Now take 	out your green thing and turn to page 37,
and right now we’re going to talk about what’s on page 37.” 	He can’t do that
because he 	ain’t 	got no page 37.
It’s even better if it’s something they get to take apart 	– 	take out that thing and
pull the red tab, 	the purple thing will fall off and in there will be… so they’re all
pulling out their shit and he 	ain’t 	got no shit.
You’ll be amazed how many people at the end 	trudge
back and get stuff because they’ve been 	embarrassed
and they’re disappointed 	and 	they’re frustrated.
If 	you 	structure it where there’s multiple breaks 	– 	so 	there’s another break and
then you come back to do more stuff 	– 	a 	bunch of them who didn’t get stuff go
get stuff because they’ve 	got to come back and sit there again with no stuff.
They either leave or they get stuff. So 	it forces purchase of stuff.
You can speak and
act as if everybody
has got the stuff.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 25 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	26
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
Live Webcasts
Live webcasts, 	you’re also striving for pressure; 	you don’t have 	as 	easy an
opportunity to create 	it. 	You still need 	it.
So the “Buy Now” 	button; 	“the operators are 	standing by now;” 	you see it 	in
direct 	response 	TV sometimes, where they show the call center. 	You’ll 	see it in 	a
DRTV 	spot, 	you’ll see it in infomercial. They 	don’t 	just say 	that 	operators are
standing by, 	they show you a bunch of operators.
In 	a telethon, 	the reason they get people on stage on camera answering the
phones 	– 	they 	could have them in another room somewhere and have more
stage for the elephants and 	the dancing girls 	– 	is it’s 	important to show you the
people who are answering the phones.
The 	dumb ones have 	them sit there with 	no phone activity instead of coaching
them, “If there’s no call coming in, 	pick up the phone and talk 	and then put it
down. It’s 	okay, 	you get to be an actress!”
So 	operators 	standing 	by 	and 	strong 	expectation, 	conveyed 	verbally 	and
visually.
On 	a webcast, 	we still need scarcity of something.
One 	of the 	things you’ll see us do, 	if you watch 	GKIC 	again and again, is you will
see the new or additional gift with purchase added segment by segment by
segment.
Now we play fair, 	so everybody
who 	bought 	at 	commercial
break 	number 	one, 	when 	we
come 	back 	towards 	the 	next
commercial 	break 	we 	add
another something 	and we give
it to everybody who bought already. It stacks so 	nobody is penalized for being
an early buyer.
Live Webcasts
 	"Buy Now” Button
 	Operators Are Standing By 	- 	NOW
 	Strong expectation, conveyed verbally
and visually
 	Stampede
 	Assumptive language 	– 	during pitch
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 26 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	27
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
But, 	every segment, 	there’s another thing added on because who’s left? 	People
who are resisting buying. 	So let’s pile on some more.
As I talked about earlier, 	you can have quasi shills, like if you’re going to do a
webcast and you’re doing it to your own base, 	you want to take extra care to
make sure some people are on the webcast 	who buy everything.
So after 	the first break, 	you 	will be able to say, “Susan from New York, thank you
Susan and congratulations and Charlie…” 	and you know them all by name.
The first ones are the quasi shills; 	they’re not fakes, 	they’re real buyers but you
made sure they were there.
Recognition of buyers by name, 	countdown of the clock 	– 	those are some of the
things you can do with the live webcast.
Presenters’ Tells
Here are the presenters’ 	tells that the audience reacts to more 	subconsciously
than consciously.
The first tell that communicates to the audience, “There’s something wrong
here, 	you probably really shouldn’t buy this etc.” is 	the presenter who has a
physiological change 	as he moves from what he thinks is content to what he
thinks is commercial.
When I got him, 	Foster was the worst I’ve ever seen in my life 	– 	absolutely 	the
worst. 	I 	see 	it 	a lot but he was the worst.
It was a 	group of chiropractors 	and Foster was on before me. I said to the
meeting planner 	at the back of the room, “He’s the best speaker I’ve ever seen in
my life,” 	which 	still today that’s true and I said, “He’s the worst platform sales
person I have ever seen. 	A 	fire hydrant could do a better job than this.”
I know 	the meeting planner 	isn’t paying him 	so 	he’s getting what he sells. 	I said,
“How 	could he possibly make a living?” 	and he wasn’t.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 27 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	28
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
He did 	content, content, content, content. He’s a tall, 	elegant, 	gentleman-looking
guy, 	nice suit; 	I mean perfect. 	Good classic orator voice.
It’s time for 	the 	call to action so here’s Foster’s call to action. The script is
horrible but 	as 	he does it he shrinks and I mean he’s 	sort of in a begging
position; his 	voice drops a couple of octaves, 	no eye 	contact, 	he’s now looking
out into space.
I mean it’s horrible. If the pitch had been any longer than the one I’m 	about to
give, 	he would be like in Wizard of Oz; eventually 	there’s a pool 	laying 	there and
he’s gone.
The 	wicked witch gets dissolved 	and 	there‘s nothing there but a green glob of
stuff. Here’s his pitch:
“Some people ask me if they can have some of my material to take home with
them. There’s some cassette programs in that suitcase at the back of the room. 	Just
take what you want and put in what you think it’s worth.”
This is his pitch. 	I’m like, “Oh my
God.”
I fixed this guy, 	by the way, 	to a
95–99% 	close rate, 	evening after
evening, after evening. 	It wasn’t
easy.
I see people doing it all the time…
not 	that 	radical 	but 	they
physiologically shift as they are
about to ask for money. Even 	if
the audience doesn’t consciously
get it, 	they subconsciously get it.
A Speaker’s or Presenter’s “Tells” that the
Audience “Reads” as Reasons NOT to Buy
 	Significant change 	– 	voice, tone, body 	–
when moving 	from ‘content’ to
‘commercial’
 	Any hint of 	apology or being apologetic
for selling
 	Self-disparaging humor when shifting
into or during selling
 	Disrespectful handling of your product
 	Hesitation or confusion
 	Hurrying the pitch vs. the preceding
presentation
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 28 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	29
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
If 	you are not okay with it as the seller, 	I know I’m not supposed
to be okay with it as the buyer. 	So I don’t buy.
Next 	tell 	is 	any hint of apology. Now, 	with the right audience 	in 	the right place,
you can be cute; 	you can let 	them in a little bit but you cannot be apologetic.
Again I see speakers doing it all the time. 	They’ll actually apologize, “Now I’m
only going to take 10 minutes of your 	time and I know you came here to learn
and I know… but I need to tell you about…”
Well what have you just told everybody? 	“What I’m doing here now is horrible,
it’s abusive, 	it’s criminal. 	I shouldn’t be doing it, but buy from me anyway.” No, 	I
don’t think so; 	I don’t think I’m going to do that.
Self-disparaging humor 	when shifting into 	the selling has the same effect as
the apology. If 	it’s in the story before the bridge, 	it’s okay. If it’s after the bridge,
it’s not okay. Because people get that it’s a cover mechanism for embarrassment
and shame about what you’re doing.
Disrespectful handling of the physical product; 	if you are going to handle it,
you 	can’t 	throw it around disrespectfully. 	I’ve seen it. I have 	to handle it lovingly
when I display it to you; 	I have to nicely put it back and make sure it’s okay
before I move on to the next one.
Confusion; 	if you are not going to memorize anything for any presentation 	–
and 	that may 	be fine by the way 	– 	memorize 	the pitch.
Because, 	if you’re uncomfortable with it at all, 	that’s the place you’re 	suddenly
going to look like an Alzheimer’s 	victim, and it’s going to be evident. 	You 	have
that deer 	in 	the 	headlights 	look; 	you’re fumbling, “Oh I forgot, 	I meant to tell you
about this…” 	You are destroying the 	thing.
In 	many instances, 	in addition to pitching, 	you should 	– 	like 	we do in the
webcasts 	– 	cut 	to a pre-canned, 	perfect CTA. 	Now 	you 	can’t be embarrassed or
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 29 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	30
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
ashamed or forgetful or confused because you’ve 	scripted it, 	you’ve 	voiced it,
you’ve 	videoed it so it is perfect.
There is no reason not 	to 	have it playing 	in a live
event either if 	you’re on a break and they’re
coming back.
Last, 	hurrying the pitch. 	People who ask the
question 	about 	the 	“boundary” 	between content
and selling, 	here’s what those people tend to do.
They 	suck up all the time on the content and they leave themselves almost no
time for the pitch and then 	they 	race through the pitch. 	The CTA itself, 	they race
through it.
Number 	one, 	what does that do? 	Well 	people don’t know what the hell 	they are
buying. The 	most important part of the presentation is that; 	that’s why you did
the rest of it 	– 	was 	to get the opportunity to sell the stuff.
So 	if you’re going to short anything, 	don’t short that.
Secondly it telegraphs to people again you don’t feel very good about this. 	“He’s
trying to get through this as quick as possible and get the hell out of here,” 	so
now they sense that and they don’t feel very good about that.
If, 	by the way, 	you have promised the 17 laws of… 	and you somehow are
running out of time, 	just skip some 	– 	but don’t put yourself in a position to hurry
the pitch.
Whatever time it is supposed to take delivered 	perfectly, 	you take that time.
Now, if you’re in a multi-speaker 	environment, 	you cannot abuse the person
behind you. If you do it in our environment, 	and I have an 	opportunity, 	I will fix
it so you never work in our world again.
If you are supposed to be done at 10.10 in a multi-speaker environment, 	you
should be done 	at 10.10. 	So 	you’ve 	got to take it out of the front. 	You 	can’t just
Whatever time it is
supposed to take
delivered perfectly,
you take that time.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 30 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	31
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
keep going until you actually get your 22 minutes of CTA. 	That’s horribly
unprofessional; 	that’s not the answer.
Format Changes
Format 	changes throughout a presentation, there is 	– 	and I’m 	mostly talking
now about video and online 	– 	direct to camera monologue, 	person talks to
camera; 	direct to camera but with an audience.
You 	do need to understand… 	if you watch 	PBS, you 	will see people make this
mistake a lot. 	You 	will see them do what I’m 	doing now. You will see them
walking back and forth and making eye contact with people in the audience and
all that.
However, 	you aren’t in the audience; 	you are at home watching it on TV.
We’re doing TV even though there’s a live audience,
therefore 	your job is to work to the camera.
You can do a little bit of this 	but you can’t do much of it. I was saved at the Peter
Lowe events early 	by a speaker 	named Pat Fripp. There’s 	10,000 people in 	there
so, 	with 	the 	lights and stuff, 	I can only see the first 	two rows then it’s just a blur.
I’m doing what I would do if I had 200 people in the room; 	I’m working the room
and I’m trying to see 	– 	is 	he with me, 	can I monkey with him, does 	she want eye
contact? That’s what 	I’m 	doing.
After one of the early ones, 	I was at San Francisco 	Airport and Pat came over
and said, “You were great, there’s one thing you don’t know 	about 	what you’re
doing.”
I said, “Great, 	tell me what I don’t
know.”
She says everybody but the first
two rows, not only can’t 	you see
Format Changes Throughout a Presentation
 	Monologue 	– 	DTC / w. Audience
 	1-to-1 interview
 	1-to-Panel interview
 	Seminar / PowerPoint presentation
 	Physical demonstration 	– 	in studio
 	Physical demonstration 	– 	in field
 	Cut to video on monitor
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 31 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	32
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
them, 	they 	can’t see you. They’re watching the Jumbotrons; 	you’re on television,
you’re not on stage.
Therefore you’re on camera; 	you’re doing TV and unfortunately you’re doing a
one camera shoot.
I 	said, 	“I’ve 	got 	it!” 	and 	I’ve 	watched 	people 	flounder 	and 	fail 	in 	that
environment often because they didn’t know what I now knew because she told
me.
You can 	usually 	improve somebody’s results the minute you fix that.
I didn’t volunteer it to everybody. 	I 	only 	volunteered 	it 	when I was getting paid
by somebody but I’m not as kind as Pat.
You automatically improve their results when you fix that because most of the
people were watching them on a big TV.
One-to-one interview, 	you can do your pitch 	person 	– 	whoever that is 	– 	and a
host, one-to-one interview.
Be 	very 	careful 	about the seats. You 	don’t 	want to 	sink, that 	is 	no good. The seats
right now on 	Kimmel’s show 	are horrendous. People 	sink into them and little
guys like Tom Cruise, 	there’s 	just 	a head.
It’s like you see in Florida when cars go past you 	and 	there’s nothing there but a
head and two little 	hands on a steering wheel. There’s nobody else there.
They’re small and they’re 	low and so those are no good.
So you’ve 	got to make sure if you do interviews 	that you have the right seats.
Most 	people, 	their energy level 	dips 	when they are comfortably seated.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 32 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	33
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
If you have somebody 	at 	one of your events who has something 	great 	to sell, 	it’s
of 	great value 	but they can’t sell for shit 	– 	maybe they can’t even speak 	for 	shit 	–
then you can do an interview on stage and you can carry that.
Now if you do too much of it, 	then you 	negate 	your ability to sell anything.
The 	one-to-panel interview 	you can do that in 	an environment obviously.
You can do the 	PowerPoint presentation. 	I will caution 	you 	about this. 	In 	a live
environment, one 	of the quickest ways 	to suck energy out of the room is to turn
on the PowerPoint presentation. Everybody’s experience with PowerPoint
speakers, 	most of them are horrible.
The 	quicker way of draining energy out of the
room 	is to 	go to 	a 	PowerPoint presentation and
dim the lights. That 	will do it.
They say, “Here comes the college professor. Uh-
oh!”
Now 	I’m not good enough to work without help,
but 	I’ll 	die 	before 	you’ll 	see 	me 	doing
PowerPoint. 	It 	just isn’t going to happen.
Physical 	demonstration 	in the studio; 	physical 	demonstration 	in 	the field; 	so
now you have to show it on video. 	And you 	can 	cut to 	video on a monitor; 	just
don’t go 	there too long.
We 	used to do it in a lot of infomercials. Now 	you 	could do it with Skype; 	you can
cut to 	Skype. Just don’t do it too long because they will decide they don’t want to
pay attention.
Length and Breakdown of Presentation
Length 	and breakdown of presentation; as 	I said, 	an infomercial is 28½ 	minutes.
One of the quickest
ways to suck energy
out of 	a 	room is to
turn on 	a
PowerPoint
presentation.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 33 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	34
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
The 	DVD sent to the home 	or 	office can be considerably 	longer as long as you
don’t bore them and all of that. I
would 	mostly 	do 	45 	to 	90
minute 	presentations 	if 	I’m
going 	to 	get 	somebody 	to
request 	the 	DVD and send them
a DVD.
Online, the 	infomercial length all
the way up to an hour is about
right.
The 	people who tell you seven
minutes, 	they’re full of crap.
As 	an example, 	when you break
it down, 	an infomercial 	is 	28½
minutes, 	a fairly typical format is you’ve 	got three segments; 	you really 	only
have 21½ 	minutes left after you take out the CTAs.
The first CTA is the longest, 	the second CTA is shorter than the first; 	the last CTA
is the shortest 	of 	everything. So you’ve 	got to break 	down your timing, 	your time
schedule, 	of any of these presentations 	and 	then 	figure out how you apportion it.
This 	is not like a sales letter… 	when you write copy 	for a sales letter, 	my
approach is I write until I’m done; 	I mean whatever it takes. If 	it is 28 pages fine,
if it is 36 pages fine, 	if it is 52 pages fine.
Here, 	we have finite time so you’ve 	got to create a time frame of your sections,
your CTAs 	and then you’ve 	got to fit things into them.
Length & Breakdown of Presentations
INFOMERCIALS
 	On TV: 28.5 minutes
 	On DVD sent to home or office: 45 to 90
 	Online: 	28.5 to 60
In 28.5:
 	1st Call to Action: 3 min.
 	2nd Call to Action: 2.5 min.
 	End of Show CTA: 1.5 min.
Leaves 21.5 divided by 3 segments
 	Long-Medium-Short:
o 	8.5/7.5/5.5
 	Medium 	– 	Long 	– 	Short:
o 	7/9/5.5
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 34 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	35
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
Webcasts
Webcasts, 	you can obviously do
three or four hours; 	we’re doing
them 	very 	successfully; 	some
people are doing longer.
Again, 	you’ve 	got 	to 	breakout
your 	CTAs, 	now 	you 	have 	X
amount of time left, 	what are
you going to do? 	Are 	you going
to do three segments? 	Are you
going four segments?
You’ve 	got to create a schematic
that you’re going to fill in the boxes.
Preview Seminars
Preview 	seminars, 	my preference is three hours. 	My 	own preference and
experience is much more than
three you shouldn’t need.
I know 	guys who are 	doing all
day, 	I 	think 	it’s 	excessive. 	In
three hours, 	you have the ability
to 	have 	a 	first 	break 	where
nobody sells anything, 	a 	second
break where all the sales is done
and they come back and you still have a shot at them at the end.
Speaking to Sell
If you’re speaking to sell, 	my own 	– 	I’m only telling you me 	but 	I can’t get it done
in 	less than 90 minutes. I wound up at 75 on the 	Success 	events and we paid 	a
price for it. 	I can’t do it 	in 	less than 90 and do it effectively.
Webcasts
3 or 4 hours
 	If 3 = 3 or 4 segments
 	If 4 = 4 or 5 segments
Egs. 4 hours = 240 minutes
 	3 CTA breaks of 8 minutes each
 	+ CTA @ end of 8 leaves 208 minutes
4 Segments:
 	Medium 	– 	Long 	– 	Short 	–Shorter
 	55/65/45/43
Preview Seminars
3 hours: 2 or 3 segments (I 	prefer 3)
Egs from SuccessTrak: 3 hours = 180 MIN
Short (no selling @ 1st 	break), Long, Short
40/10 Min Break/60/15 Min Pitch/15 Min
Break/30/10 min. end pitch if needed
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 35 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	36
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
For 	a product like 	Magnetic 	Marketing, 	if you go back through 	what we’ve 	talked
about 	here, there are 	certain things you have to know. 	There are certain things
you have to have agreed to in sequence before you will buy that product.
If I 	have to cheat any of that, 	you really can’t buy the product because we haven’t
had 	the sequential agreement. 	I
prefer 	two 	hours 	without 	a
break.
Three to three and a half, 	I’ll
stick a 	break in 	it 	because 	it
allows me to create more than
one 	selling 	break 	or 	a 	non-
selling break and then a break.
If 	you 	are 	speaking 	for 	other
people by the way, 	you’ve 	got to fight for what works for you.
I just 	got an engagement 	where they 	only want to give 	me 	90 minutes. 	I said,
“No, 	I want two hours. Screw somebody else. 	I don’t care about them, 	I care
about me. 	I want two hours.” 	So 	I got two hours.
Video: Production Standards, Options and Advice
If 	you’re doing video please think about some of these 	things.
Studio
If 	you’re doing it in studio, 	really think through whether you want to do green
screen or whether you want to build sets.
Nothing you do on green screen looks as good as a real set. Very 	little is done on
TV on green screens even today; 	they have sets. You 	can actually go to
Hollywood and see them.
There’s a richness to 	it, there’s 	a 	depth to it, 	there’s objects in 	it; 	dressing 	a 	set in
a way that symbolizes things and is meaningful to people and adds some
interest to what’s going 	on 	is important.
Speech to Sell
90 MIN, NO BREAK
2 HRS., BREAK QUESTIONABLE (Net Gain Only 15
Min.)
3 OR 3.5 HRS, BREAK
3.5 OR 4 	– 	POSSIBLY 2 BREAKS (1st Not Selling)
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 36 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	37
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
Locations
There’s 	lots of choices for locations besides the 	studio. 	You 	might shoot in your
house; 	you might shoot in your office; 	you can go to 	a 	factory, 	to a place, 	to a
hotel to an iconic site.
If you’re doing a health pitch, 	you
can go stand in front of the 	Male
clinic… 	until they catch you!
If 	you’re doing a 	Get 	Rich thing, 	you
can go stand in front of 	the 	Statue of
Liberty 	and 	you 	can 	talk 	about
financial liberty.
There’s iconic sites that, 	at least for
a short time, 	you can go 	and 	film
there 	quick 	and 	get 	out 	before
anybody asks you about permits or anything of 	that 	other stuff.
Planes, trains, boats, automobiles 	– 	there’s 	Seinfeld’s 	show now of comedians
drinking coffee 	in 	cars; 	they sit in a car and talk to each other and drink coffee
and people are tuning in and watching it on 	a 	webcast.
Outdoors shoots 	add 	a lot of interest and they can allow you 	to make a point and
to symbolize things. 	I will tell you they are giant pain in the butt.
You’ve 	got to allow some 	time and you’ve 	got to have people who know what
they’re doing.
They 	are pretty painful most of the time. I shot an infomercial 	– 	you’ve never
seen it and you won’t 	– 	with Garry 	Halbert in Key West onsite. What a horror
show!
Of course it is Key West, 	so no member of the public is going to be cooperative
with this at all.
Production Standards, Options & Advice
Studio
 	Sets(s)
 	Green Screen
Locations
 	Homes & Offices
 	Plant, Factory, 	Office Tour
 	Hotels
 	Iconic or Symbolic Sites
 	Planes, Trains, Boats and Automobiles
 	Outdoor
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 37 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	38
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
They 	are going to engage and a lot of people… 	not the tourists 	but the 	people
who live in Key West… 	are pretty much there because they’re not allowed to live
anywhere else. This is like the last stop for them other than a prison… 	that’s
who’s there.
So 	a number of them are pretty high and a number of them are pretty drunk all
day long. It’s hot, 	it’s humid.
We 	shot one scene 	with 	Halbert and the host 	– 	who 	was 	Don Drysdale, who’s
dead now, 	the baseball player 	– 	in 	big high canvas chairs on the marina with the
yachts behind and seagulls.
This is 	like five 	minutes; 	I need two 	questions, 	two answers 	– 	that’s 	all I need. 	It
took me all day.
Just about the time you get it, 	a seagull poops right on his head. 	I’m 	down to like
three words as, on 	the yacht right behind 	Drysdale, 	somebody hits the button
and disgorges the sewage. We’ve got to start over.
Outdoor stuff is just ugly but it adds 	an 	enormous
amount of interest to a video.
When 	you go to people’s natural habitats 	and shoot there, 	you’ve 	just got to
decide how much pain you’re willing to endure.
Activity
The live seminar 	stuff with audiences works. 	Just 	make sure you’ve 	got multiple
cameras so you’ve 	got stuff to cover, and you shoot everybody laughing and
everybody clapping.
If you’re going to shoot over a long period of time, 	you’ve 	got to get them back in
the seats they were in after they go to the bathroom because if they switch seats
now you’ve 	got problems.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 38 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	39
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
You can’t let anybody leave early; 	they’ve 	got to be committed to be there
because then you’ve 	got empty seats so you’ve 	got 	to 	think through the audience
thing.
For technical procedural stuff 	– 	an 	activity where they’re going to watch you do
something 	– 	you’ve 	got to allow enough time to shoot 	that 	at 56 different angles
and ways and all of that.
Filming
There’s 	one-camera 	shoots,
which is what most people do
for 	web 	stuff; 	two-camera
shoots; 	three-camera 	shoots;
four-camera shoots.
There’s 	stationary and moving. If
you 	move 	while 	you 	get
recorded, 	sometimes you can’t
do it.
Some 	people can’t walk and talk
at 	the 	same 	time, 	it’s
problematic 	for 	them; 	other
people can do it really well. 	If 	you’re filming an old person sometimes that’s not
good, because 	they 	sort of shuffle.
A 	boom mic, 	if you can afford it, 	is a great thing to have in a studio shoot 	because
you get an extra moving camera angle when you go into post production.
Extra shots to use in post 	– 	the 	biggest thing I’ll tell 	you, if you were shooting
something that you’re not just going to live broadcast, 	or you’re not just going to
slap 	up 	on the web as a one-and-done, you’re actually shooting something that
you want to live with for a while, all your great work is going to be done in post.
Production Standards, Options & Advice
Activity
 	“Live” Seminar/With Audience
 	Demonstrations
 	Technical-Procedural
Filming
 	1, 2, 3 or 4 Camera Shoots
 	Stationary or Moving
 	Boom
 	Extra Shots to use in Post
 	Product Beauty Shots 	– 	Active, Passive
B-Roll
 	Product Use
 	Lifestyle
 	Places
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 39 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	40
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
Post production is where everything happens and
you can’t have enough footage.
Just about the time you think you’ve 	got it right, 	shoot 	some 	more.
Get 	the boats with nobody sitting in front of them; get the boat moving in; 	get
the boat moving out.
Get 	cover shot, 	cover shot, 	cover shot, cover shot 	because he may not ever get
the two sentences together the way we need them; 	but he’ll have all the pieces
and 	if you don’t have anything to cover him with, 	while you continue to hear the
sound, 	you are screwed.
You’re stuck with what’s called a sharp cut 	– 	you
can see it, 	I cut it and just slapped 	it together and
took six words out. It’s visible, 	there 	needs to be
cover 	– 	the 	boat 	(and 	you’ve 	got to be able to
come back), 	the other person.
That’s 	why three-camera 	shoots 	are better. 	I’ve
got one stationary wide shot, I’ve 	got a close up
on Ben and I’ve 	got a close 	up 	on 	the other person 	for the whole time, so I wind
up with the ability to cover a couple of different ways.
Product beauty shots, 	shoot the stuff you’re selling; active 	– 	somebody handling
it with nice hands, moving it around 	– 	stationary, 	get a bunch of product beauty
shots.
B-Roll
Get a lot of B-roll. 	Get 	the product used if there is a way to use it; 	if it is a note
book with pages in it, 	get shots 	of 	somebody opening the note book looking
through pages.
If they’re going to listen to CDs, 	get some video of them taking the CD out of the
package 	and 	putting 	it in a CD player.
Make 	sure you get
every possible thing
you can ever want
from 	your 	celebrity
now.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 40 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	41
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
You won’t know you want it necessarily until you get to 	post
but you can’t 	go back and get most of this.
If there is a celebrity, if you rented a celebrity, 	make sure you get every possible
thing you can ever want from that celebrity now because you can’t get him back.
If you want them to handle the product, 	you’ve 	got to think through all of that.
Seminar or Studio Audience
If 	using a seminar or a studio audience, you can 	use 	volunteers, 	you can rent to
order.
You do want to try and match the audience if they’re going to be visible to your
audience 	who 	is going to be buying.
There’s no point in having people in 	the 	visible audience
who don’t match up with your buyers.
If you’re selling weight loss to
women, 	there’s 	no 	reason 	to
have a guy in the audience. 	It’s
stupid. You’ve 	got to know who
your market is.
If you are, candidly, 	selling 	and
90% of your customers and 90%
of the people you are driving to
the 	webcast 	are 	white, 	you’re
not 	going to get the diversity
award from your webcast. 	There
is no reason to have a black guy
and an Asian and an American Indian with a feather; 	there’s no reason to have
any of them.
If Using 	a 	Seminar or Studio Audience
 	Volunteers
 	Rent-To-Order
CONSIDER DIVERSITY &/OR MATCH TO
BUYERS
 	More Than You Need
 	Clear and 	Complete Instructions
 	Assigned Babysitter
 	Extra Wardrobe On Hand
MAKE THEM HAPPY
 	Presenter(s) Must ‘Play Well’ 	with
People
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 41 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	42
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
Vice versa, 	if 	a 	significant number of customers 	or viewers 	are 	black, 	you can’t
have all white folks in the audience.
They 	have 	got to see themselves.
If all your 	buyers are 55 and over and you’ve 	got 20 year olds in there because
your kid and three of his buddies were available to be in the audience, 	bad idea.
So you want to match to your buyer.
Get 	more than you need. 	Some 	of them won’t show up; 	some of them, 	which is
really bad 	and you’ve 	got to wedge them in the back, but 	some of them will leave
early even though they agreed not to leave early.
Some of them will get sick. 	Somebody 	will be in
your 	audience 	and 	they’ll 	be 	scratching
themselves the whole time, and 	you’re going to
have to get them out there and replace them.
Clear 	and 	complete 	instructions 	before 	they
come and a babysitter; 	do not have an audience
without at least one person assigned to manage
that audience.
They’re not 	Buicks 	that you park in position, 	put the parking gear on and let
them idle; 	that’s not how human beings are.
Have extra wardrobe on hand for them. 	Despite your instructions, 	he’s going to
show 	up in a purple polka 	dot sweatshirt that says FU 	on it and, 	when you turn
it inside out, 	it says FU inside it. You need to be able to dress that 	guy.
The 	first thing he’s going 	to do when he gets there, he’s 	got a white shirt on and
he’s going to spill 	his 	coffee all over it. 	So you’ve 	got to have extra wardrobe.
Make them happy.
Do 	not have an
audience without at
least one person
assigned to manage
that audience.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 42 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	43
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
Audiences need to be happy;
so you’ve 	got to make them happy.
The babysitter has to be able to interact with them. You 	need to come out and
engage them.
The presenter has to 	play well with the people.
The 	worst presenter in the world to put 	with 	a
live audience is somebody that doesn’t play well
with the people.
We’re shooting this show… I won’t name him, a
famous 	speaker 	you 	would 	know… 	we’re
shooting a live audience show and we have
stocked it all with his real testimonials at great
difficulty.
There’s 	250 people in this audience; 	the whole environment looks 	like an Oprah
show or something.
He’s on stage doing his thing and act two is interaction with the audience.
The 	host, 	who is the celebrity, 	is like Phil Donahue used to be so he is out in 	the
audience with the microphone. 	This 	is what’s going 	on.
This 	woman stands up and starts telling her story, 	she’s 	excited and crying and…
this is not choreographed, 	you couldn’t choreograph it, 	you couldn’t 	pay enough.
She 	pushes herself past the celebrity, 	she runs to the stage to give our guy a big
hug and he 	steps 	back
You’ll never get it again and the truth 	is 	he don’t like anybody and he don’t like
his customers and he don’t like physical 	contact.
It’s better not to make him interact with a live audience.
The worst presenter
in the world to put
with a live audience
is somebody that
doesn’t play well
with the people.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 43 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	44
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
Dealing with Inexperienced Presenters
If 	you are dealing 	with inexperienced presenters, be very careful about doing
cue 	cards or prompters.
They lock on them and you can tell they’re locked on them; 	you can tell… 	they
follow the bobbing ball.
It’s very hard to disengage them
and fix that.
You 	are far better off… 	if you’ve
got 	an 	experienced 	host, 	put
him 	on 	prompter 	but 	just
overshoot.
Just shoot 	a 	ton with 	them and clean it up in post.
Be 	very careful about asking amateurs to use pro 	tools.
You’re better off letting them sit at the tall table on the stools with notes 	– 	and
everybody sees they’re dealing with notes 	– 	than 	you are having them tuned
into the prompter.
Don’t make demands they’re not going to be able to do. 	Know 	what you’ve 	got to
deal with if you’re shooting testimonials, 	if you’re shooting another presenter.
Limit the rehearsal if you’re dealing with 	inexperienced presenters. 	They tend
to get worse not better, one run
through and then 	take what you
can get.
If using Pro Presenters
 	Define, agree to & meet or exceed their
requirements
 	Create 	a time and way to “make friends”
 	Know or sensibly guess their limitations
 	“Massage” beyond contract 	– 	if so, get
new signed release
 	Be respectful and pleasant, but not
deferential
 	Good idea to have a “bad guy” on your
team
If Inexperienced Presenters
 	NO Prompter or Cue Cards 	– 	better to
have visible notes
 	Don’t make impractical demands
 	Limited rehearsal
 	Allow extra time
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 44 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	45
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
Using Professional Presenters
If you’re using professional presenters, 	then it’s more about real management of
them.
If 	they’re hired, 	if they’re showing up to shoot, you need time to befriend them 	–
so 	they like you.
A little-known secret about celebrities is 	they’re not treated very well and so
they show up braced 	for a really bad experience with a jackass.
That’s their attitude coming in the door and often their behavior will reflect it,
unless you have fixed that.
Live Broadcasts 	and Edit
If 	live 	to broadcast, webcast and you’re really serious about what you’re doing
and/or 	there 	are 	regulatory
issues with what you’re doing,
you may want 	tape 	delay just
like in live television.
You do want live switch, 	which
means some of the editing is
done as you go.
People who are good at it can do a lot of it. 	If
you’re booking a studio, 	talk to them about their
live switch capabilities.
You want to do post and plan to get more 	than
one use out of whatever you do.
What 	if the first use doesn’t work? When we took money from people 	to
produce 	an 	infomercial, I always strategized, “Let’s 	make sure we get web video
that you’ll be able to use on the web. Can 	we get a DVD out of this? 	Can we get
five little videos you’ll be able to use?”
Plan 	to get more
than one use out of
whatever you do.
Edit
 	If Live to Broadcast/Webcast, consider
tape delay
 	Live-Switch
 	Quality Post-Production (4X to 8X ratio)
– 	from multiple takes
 	Get more than 	one ‘product’/use
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 45 of 46 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	46
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 4
What’s all the different stuff we can get out of this day of production, 	two days of
production, 	whatever, so if 	the main thing you did turns out to be a clinker you
have other opportunities to do things with it?
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 46 of 46 --
